summary: The candidate designs Spotify, focusing on finding and playing music. They discuss initial system components (app, web server, load balancer, databases), data storage (splitting audio blobs from metadata), and then delve into optimizations like multi-layer caching (CDN, web server memory, local app storage) to handle hot songs and scale. Finally, they touch upon load balancing metrics for streaming data and geo-aware replication for global distribution.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Hello there and welcome to this system design mock interview. Today, we want to show you a really high-quality, best-in-class system design answer so that you can use it to prepare for your interviews and know exactly what standard you should be aiming for. With us, the candidate today, I have a former engineering manager at Google, Mark. Mark, how are you doing?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I'm doing great, Tom. Thanks for having me. I'm looking forward to doing this.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Oh, thanks for coming on. It's great to have you. Before we get started, I just want to quickly give people an idea of your background as an engineering manager.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, I was an engineering manager at Google for 13 years and worked on several large-scale systems with lots of great engineers. So, I hope I've learned a little bit from them, and it'll be interesting to see me on the other side of the fence, being the interview candidate here. So, I'm looking forward to it.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: I'm sure you'll do a great job. And Mark also is a coach on our platform, so if you need some expert feedback ahead of your interview at Google, Facebook, anywhere, any tech company, then do check him out on our platform. Okay, if you're ready, Mark, let's crack straight into it. The question I want to ask you today is: How would you design Spotify?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, so yeah, Spotify is probably one of the most popular music streaming services out there, I guess. So, let me think about that just for a moment. Because there are lots, there are a lot of aspects of Spotify here. Let me see if I can actually, maybe I'll just take some notes here so that I can kind of think through this, if that's okay. And can I make some assumptions? So, I have my notes. I'll just put this right in here, and then if it's okay, I'll just use this for notes as well as sort of the design diagrams and things like that later on.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, sounds good.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So yeah, let's talk about some different aspects of Spotify. For Spotify, I can think of things. I mean, obviously, there are songs, music—that's the main thing. There are playlists, users, artists. I guess Spotify even has things like podcasts that you can listen to and things like that. So, there's a lot to it. In terms of the system design for today, can we constrain this a little bit just so that we can actually get through a design? What would be good to focus on here?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, let's constrain it. Let's limit ourselves to finding and playing music. Okay?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, so use cases: finding and playing music. I'm just going to write it down verbatim here. Great. Yeah, I think that's enough to talk about. Okay, good. So, if I think about that, let me try and ask a little bit or think a little bit about the use cases a little bit more. My typical use case then, as a user, if I'm signed up to Spotify and I have an app on a phone, I am going to browse some music. Maybe I'll search for a particular song. But then the most core thing is that I play a song, and it's coming back through my phone, maybe my car, or wherever I might be, headphones, to listen to it with.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Big, broad questions like this, you need to constrain the problem, make it solvable in the space of an hour. And Mark did this well, establishing the use cases we wanted to focus on. If you're asked to design an already existing system, in this case Spotify, share what you know about it with the interviewer. They can correct you or fill you in if you have any knowledge gaps. So, if I think about other, if I think about metrics here, let's think about size. Let's do some quick drilling down into some numbers here. And pardon me while I get this going here. So, in numbers, tell me about how many users you're thinking about here for this design?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, let's say a billion users.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, one billion users. Okay, what about the number of songs?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I think I saw it somewhere that Spotify has about 80 million songs, but let's say we need to have capacity for 100 million on there.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, all right. So, 100 million songs, a billion users, and we're going to focus on the song piece of it. But let me do a little bit of math here, or a little bit of thinking, and I'll just double-check with you to make sure that this makes sense. From what I know, a typical MP3 audio for a typical song is about five megabytes. I mean, I think that depends on the encoding of the song, how long the song is, and things like that. But if that's okay, we can just kind of make that assumption. Is that fair?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that sounds good to me.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, I mean, I think you can encode these things at really low quality, like 96 kilobits per second, all the way up to 320 kilobits. But right in the middle, 128-160 is about maybe about that. So, all right, let's assume that. And so then, let me just extrapolate from this. If you've got five megabytes per song, you've got 100 million songs. So, I think that translates to—I'm trying to do my math here—100 million would be going to a thousand times that would be 100 billion, and then 100 trillion would be a million times times five would be 500 trillion. So, we're talking about total audio is something like 500 terabytes, which is, I guess, the same thing as half a petabyte of data. And so that's, I'm making that assumption. And so then, depending on how you replicate this data, because you typically want to have these songs in multiple, have multiple copies of these things so they don't get lost, etc., and if some replica is down. So, maybe you do, let's say, 3x replication. So, that would mean like one and a half petabytes of raw audio data. And then, each song in terms of the metadata, meaning the song title and things like that, and artists and all these things, the metadata is probably not very big. It might be, I don't know, 100 bytes per song or something like that per song of metadata. And so, you might wind up with, okay, so let me do the—you have it, and that's a billion, 10 billion. So, that's only 10 gigabytes of song metadata. It's not really very much. And even if you say it's a kilobyte, then that's 100 gigabytes. So, it's not very much. And let me just double-check my math here: 100 million times 100, really 100 bytes? Yeah, I think that would be 10 gigabytes. So, even if we said, if we rounded it up dramatically and said 100 gigabytes, that's just not a ton of data. Now, in terms of users, user data, you might have, again, maybe you have a kilobyte per user metadata. I guess it's all metadata. And so, that might be times a billion users, so that would be a terabyte of data approximately. So, this is just me doing some quick metrics, quick calculations to get a rough back-of-the-envelope of how big these things are. So, does that make sense so far? Does that seem?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that all makes sense so far.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Before starting your high-level design, as Mark did, get some metrics to help you identify any higher-level decisions that might be influenced by the scale of the system. Mark asked the right questions about the number of users and number of songs. He made some rough calculations without getting bogged down in the numbers. What Mark didn't calculate was the traffic—how many songs are streamed per day or per second. Even that would have helped him figure out the scaling of the web servers, how many he'd need, and how much bandwidth he'd need. However, since this didn't impact his high-level design, it was okay not to go into the details of scaling at this point.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, so let me maybe think about some basic components here, and I'm going to draw them, but I'll get into them maybe a little bit more in detail later. Okay, so some basic components that I would see. Let me start with something that is my best attempt at a mobile phone here. So, you've got your Spotify app, and I'll make this smaller so it fits into the thing. So, you've got your app, which is on your phone typically, and I'm going to assume here that we're talking about the phone app because that's very common. And so, you've got your phone. The phone is going to be talking to an application server, a Spotify application server somewhere, or web server, I guess. And so, let's draw some. Let me just put a like over here, just say there's a Spotify web server. And there's not going to just be one; there's going to be lots of them, and assume that that's a lot. And then, of course, just because this is a standard thing that you do when you have applications in the cloud here—this is, I guess, part of the cloud—you've got a load balancer. And so, let me draw. Let's assume that we've got an arrow here that's talking. So, the Spotify app is talking through the load balancer ultimately to these web servers. And so, those are some key components here. Let me think about this a little bit further. Yeah, I think that's good so far.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, and then, of course, the most important thing, or one of the most important things here, let me see if I can find a—I know that there's a shape here, here we go, that's a good shape. I'm just going to say, call this the database. And let me make this a little bit more. Let's see, that's what I wanted. Okay, so there's a database. And so, the web servers are going to be talking to the database and basically reading information, writing information to and from the database. So, those are kind of the very high-level components.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: In system design interviews, good dual-bandwidth communication is important. That's to say, you need to communicate well via both drawing and speaking. You should practice this thoroughly before your interview. Mark did this well; he started drawing the components quite early on in his answer, and this allowed the interviewer to follow along easily. It's okay to have simpler and fewer components at this point in the interview; you can then break it out later in your answer, as Mark did when he splits his database in two.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: And I probably am thinking about this. I think this is obviously oversimplified, and I'm not going to redesign Spotify, you know, the existing full application as it exists. But I do think I can get a little bit more detailed here. I'm thinking about the data again because the data is important. So, I'm thinking about the metadata and the user data and the audio data, and they're very different types of data. So, I'm going to actually split this database here into two things, and I'll explain that, or I'll try to explain them just a little bit. Come on, there we go, make this just a little bit more. So, I'm going to split this up and say that there's a song audio database and then there's going to be a metadata database. So, this is users, songs, what else? I mean, ultimately, there'll probably be artists and etcetera, things like that, but we're focusing on the songs, I think, and the music and so on. This is also a database, of course, song audio database and metadata. And so, I think, let me make this a little bit more. This Spotify web server is actually going to talk to this, to the meta—oops—the metadata and the audio database. It's actually going to be talking to two databases here.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that's interesting. Can you go into a bit more detail about why you'd split them into two?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, I'm kind of making that assumption. So, okay, let me try and think of this in terms of the technologies that I might use. I might use something like Amazon S3 here, and I might use Amazon RDS. And so now, let me explain what I mean by that. The type of data that the actual MP3 files—those I think of as immutable data. They're just blobs of data, they're files, they're five megabytes roughly on average. And they're not going to change; they're just being streamed, they're being stored and streamed essentially. And so, a blob database, which is what S3 is, lends itself really well towards that. And it could be something like Google Drive, or there are other technologies that are sort of just document-like blob storage systems. But Amazon S3 lends itself really well to that, and it scales greatly. You can just add more and more and more, and it scales linearly, which is great. And so, then you can connect it up to be able to stream the data and things like that. So, that type of data and the access patterns for that, which is really mostly read—you're just streaming this data, you're never going back and forth and writing to it—that would be why I would want to put the songs in that kind of a database. Now, S3 is great for that, works really well. But the metadata, which is the users and their information, and the songs and their information, and maybe they get updated, maybe you're searching across them and having to do queries to find songs for a particular genre or artists or things like that, or you're trying to update your users—like if I'm playing a song and I want to remember where I left off so that when I continue playing it, it remembers that—I'm going to be modifying the data quite a bit or going back and forth between that database and doing these queries. And you can't really do queries over S3, but you can over like a relational database. So, MySQL would be another option. I just chose RDS here, really Amazon's relational database, because I'm going with the AWS family here. But yeah, so let me try and just write something here a little bit more in terms of songs. You would have things like a song ID, you would have a song, maybe a URL like that you'd use for sharing, you would have an artist, a genre, maybe there's a link to album cover, and maybe there's the link to the audio, I guess. So, this is too big to fit. So, this is the type of data that would be stored in—let me draw this in here again—like in that database. And then just the raw—I mean, I almost don't need to write this here—so, song MP3 would be stored in this database down here. So, yeah, I think that the access patterns, the types of things that you want to do, the size of the data—because the size of the data here, I think we said, was going to be, well, half a petabyte for one replica—so S3 lends itself well to that. The data here is going to be in the maybe terabyte range, something like that, and it's going to have lots of queries over it, maybe some updates and things like that. So, that's why I would separate these out, is because I think the access patterns, the size of the data, and the type of queries that you need to be able to do over would be very different. Does that make sense?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yes, that answers it. Yeah, it does. Thank you. Yeah, I think that makes sense. At this point of your answer, when you've laid out the main components, it's good to start identifying some technologies, as Mark did here, starting with the database. His design made sense, breaking up the databases into audio data and metadata, but he could have explained why he was doing this upfront rather than waiting for the interviewer to ask why. In general, try to be upfront about your decision-making.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, feel free to continue. So, okay, let's say we have these two databases, and I'm just trying to think about now the actual use cases we talked about: finding and playing music. So, for finding music, I think the Spotify app would need to request to find music. And so, the user probably either is typing in an artist's name, or maybe they're selecting a bunch of filters. I actually am not that familiar with how possible that is. But let's say I'm searching for music for an artist with a particular genre. And so, ultimately, somehow I'm filling in this query in my app, either by clicking on some things or typing some things or a combination. And then that request to find music is going to be sent to this web server, going through the load balancer to pick a web server. It's going to go there, and then that web server is going to issue a translated query to the relational database to find a bunch of songs and return a list of songs and return that back up to the app with whatever metadata there is. And let's say that it finds, I don't know, 100 songs that match my request for Korean pop music or something like that. And so, I now get back 100 songs, and I can look at those. And now, once I've gotten that list of songs—and by the way, for that query where I'm searching for music, I don't touch the MP3s at all. I don't need to go to that database at all. I just need to go to the metadata database and do this query. And because it's a relational database, that query can happen pretty efficiently, etc. So, now I return that information back, and then I display that to the user. Does that seem, does that make sense in terms of finding music?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that makes sense. That's good.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, and so now I have a list of songs, and maybe one of the ones in that list is like, 'Oh yeah, that's what I was looking for.' And so, I click on that song to play it. I want to hear that song. So, that's that second part of the use case that we talked about, which is playing music. So, now to play music, this gets a little interesting, and this is, I think, I'm going to wind up maybe changing what I'm thinking about here a little bit. But let me just talk through it. So, I click on the play button, or I click on the song to play it, and that translates into a request from the app to the web server to start playing a song. It has an ID, like I mentioned, an ID in it. And so now, the web server, based on that song information ID, maybe has to go to this database here to look up the link—what did I call it? Oh, an audio link. Maybe that's an MP3 link, I don't know, something like that, that's a better term for it, I don't know. So, the MP3 link or audio link would be returned. And now, the Spotify web server has to go to the database where the actual audio is stored and fetch that database. Now, you could imagine streaming that five megabytes from this database, so chunk by chunk it comes back, and chunk by chunk it goes up to the Spotify app. In order to do this, you need like a WebSocket connection, so you need a kind of a long-standing connection between the application and this web server so that you can send the data back in chunks. But I'm not sure whether I would need to do that, or whether, because it's only five megabytes, that's possibly small enough to just fit in memory, you know, read from S3 and fit into memory, and then have that particular web server chunk it back from there. I think that might be better because it would eliminate the possible lag between the database, so you don't start streaming the audio until you have it in memory. That might be something that I would consider doing. So now, let me think about, so that would be—does that make sense in terms of the playing? Like, you start playing, and obviously, you can control it, you can pause playing, and so on. But does that make sense? So, you start playing, the web server gets the request to play it, maybe gets the information about where to go over here to get the S3 audio storage, reads that back. It's only five megabytes; that should not take—it should be almost instantaneous. And then it starts streaming that back to the application. Does that make sense?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that makes sense. Please carry on.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, so all good so far. Okay, so this almost sounds too good to be true, or sounds too easy. I think it's got to be more complicated than that. Okay, so one thing I'm just realizing here is, probably out of these, what did we say? We said 100 million songs. There's probably a lot of stuff that is—I'm going to use the term 'indie artists' or something like that—stuff that few people, not very many people, listen to or isn't very popular. And on the flip side of that, I'm thinking about what could go wrong here. Well, if, like BTS, which is a Korean pop band, if they, let's say, they release a new song, and it's like, 'Hey, everybody wants to listen to this, this is super, oh, have you heard the latest song?' etc. If you've got all of these web servers all fetching that same thing, that same song, and let's say you've got requests like it's released, and in the next minute, you've got, I don't know, how many? You said a billion users. Okay, let's say that 10 million users all request the song all at the same time or something like that because it's just been released, and they're following social media. You could easily overload, in terms of bottlenecks, you could possibly overload that bucket, or that bucket, excuse me, that particular song, that file, or whatever, however it's stored in AWS. And it could be stored different ways. I'm using S3 here. You could overload AWS or S3 there, and you could also possibly be, you know, loading up all these web servers with the same song, streaming the same thing back. So, it's a lot of bandwidth, etc. So, a better thing to do, and a common thing to do for stuff like this, is to use what's called a CDN, Content Delivery Network, which is like a cache. So, this helps reduce the amount of load on backends. So, let me draw something here. Let me just—I am going to pick what shape am I going to pick? I'll just pick randomly something like this. So, maybe over here, so I'm going to say here this is a CDN, which is a song audio cache. This is typing in. And so, what would happen? So, and by the way, the technologies here, just to—this CDN, a Content Delivery Network, is usually very, very close in terms of number of hops and network connections to users. So, what would happen is, let me draw another arrow here, and let me draw an arrow down to here. So, what would happen here is that the first time that this song, this new BTS newly released single, is requested, the web server would read it and stream it like normal. But probably, we need to make sure that these Spotify web servers are somewhat—they're keeping track of things, and they're keeping track of, you know, which songs are being requested, which ones are hot. They probably have a heat map, the most recently requested songs. And so, in that heat map, as they see, 'Oh, this song is now, I've seen this requested the fifth, third time in a minute or something like that,' at that point in time, what it might do is it might actually, instead of just streaming it back to itself, or copying it back to itself, excuse me, it might actually load that into the CDN. And so, I am trying to—I'm going to have to draw another arrow, I think, in order to do that, because these things probably have to talk to the CDN. And by the way, I'm not a super expert on this area in terms of how this works, but there's some connection between this edge caching, this Content Delivery Network, which is just there for caching, and these Spotify web servers. So, these web servers are going to somehow notify the CDN, 'Hey, you should be pulling this song, this BTS latest song. Pull that from the MP3, the audio storage.' So, the CDN would load that up. And this now has to work in conjunction with the application. So, that means that the application, when the person decides, when I request the song, 'I want to play this latest BTS song,' and I haven't had it on my phone yet, it's just been released, before I go and I talk to the web server, I might even go and check in this—the application might actually go and check in this Content Delivery Network to see, 'Is it there?' It might also be so, and if it is there, then I just read it from there. The other option, or the possibility—and again, this is technology that I'm sort of not super familiar with—is that it goes and asks the web server just like it normally would, and the web server sends back a redirect saying, 'Hey, I don't have it, but you should go over here to this Content Delivery Network, this edge cache, to fetch the song. It's much closer to you, you'll get better performance, and you won't be loading me up so much.' So, I've got a lot of arrows here, but the standard flow for a song, first time around, is coming around, getting the metadata, going around to reading the audio data. The web server is now keeping track and realizing, 'Oh, you know what, this is getting—I'm getting multiple requests for this same song.' Now, I'm going to tell the CDN, 'Go and fetch this song.' It's going to fetch it. And so now, the application can be redirected, for example, to read that audio from there. So, that's a lot of talking, but I was realizing that caching here seems like it's a very important point in this design and would help with the bottleneck of hot songs, if you will. Does that, is that?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Absolutely. Yeah, by the way, that's a really good answer.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Okay, and there's, again, in the AWS family here—I'm going to use the family—obviously, if I were a Googler, I might not be using this. But there's a technology called CloudFront, and CloudFront is basically a Content Delivery Network. There are a bunch of other ones—gosh, Fastly, is that what it is? I can't remember what the names of these technologies are, but there are a bunch of other technologies that are very similar in nature that allow you to do this. So, that would help with the bottlenecks. And in terms of the caching here, you could also—and this is part of the reason, by the way, I'm just stepping backwards here a little bit—part of the reason why I think it would be better initially when the web server is getting the song for the first time, for it to read the entire MP3 into its memory. Because then, if it is getting multiple requests for a particular song, then it doesn't have to go to the database; it can actually just feed it from its memory. And that's also a form of caching, of course. So, it's got a local cache essentially of these songs. And you could imagine having a shared cache that's shared across these web servers, so that could be another optimization. But my point is that if we have a cache in memory in these web servers that stores the songs, then we're offloading the database a little bit. If we then have the cache here at the edge of the network—so this is, that's what it's called, edge network, edge caching—then we're offloading the web servers to a large extent because the streaming is optimized for streaming and so on. And I think if we go one step further, actually, I'm sure, and if I were designing it, I would design the application, because these are on smartphones, to store the songs that are played frequently by the specific user locally in the local storage. So, it's another form of caching. So then, if it finds the song in its local cache, essentially local store here on the phone, then you wouldn't even need to go to the network at all, right? So, you could actually—I'll even draw like this—so it's like a little cache, a little local storage here of songs. So, there's multi-layer caching, I guess what I'm saying, multiple levels of caching, ultimately with the goal to provide the best user experience, make sure it's super fast, and to then offload obviously the system to allow it to scale and limit costs, etc. So, that's probably enough on caching, and I think I've gone deep a little bit there.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Be ready for the interviewer to ask you about particular components or different aspects of your system and to talk in detail about them. It's also good to talk through the flow of a particular use case. This helps make sure the interviewer is clear on it, but also helps you test your design as you talk through it, as well as helping you identify any potential bottlenecks, as it did with Mark here. Mark explained the purpose of the caching and also where caches might exist in the system. Caching wasn't limited to the CDN and the app, but also the web server. He could have talked about caching the metadata as well as the song data. We've done caching, that's all good. Now, I wanted to ask you about load balancing. How would you think about load balancing for this particular app?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, magic load balancing. It's, yeah, a little bit hand-wavy here. I think of load balancing—I mean, so load balancing commonly is used to—the load balancer's job here really is to make sure that these web servers are not overloaded so that they're providing good service to the end users and they're not overloaded. And overloaded can mean many things. Often, it's in terms of CPU. So, you're getting lots of requests coming in, and if you're getting lots of requests coming in, then just load balancing these requests across the servers so that there's roughly the same number of requests on each one, assuming they're all equal, would probably also result in the CPU utilization being about equal. I think for this application, I would think about load balancing, I might think about it a little bit differently because we're streaming data. And so, instead of using CPU as my load balancing metric to figure out how to distribute the load, I might be looking at possibly network bandwidth. Because if a particular web server is not doing a lot from a CPU perspective, but if it is I/O bound or network bound, meaning it's hitting its limit of its network connection, then I wouldn't want the load balancer to send it more traffic because it's just going to bog down, and you're going to get skips and things like that. So, I might make this load balancer aware of multiple metrics, one of them being network, maybe memory for caching, although you can kind of limit that possibly, but maybe not CPU for this purpose. It might be requests outstanding or current streams or something like that. There might be some other metrics, but I would want to make this load balancer maybe a little bit smarter than just a typical request round-robin load balancing scheme. So, I'm not sure if that's getting at what you're asking.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I think that answers my question. Load balancing is not a one-size-fits-all. Mark mentioned looking at different metrics as a way to load balance across different servers, which was a clever approach. Mark was very open about the fact that load balancing isn't an area he's an expert on, and this is okay. It's fine to be honest when you're asked about things that are at the limits of your knowledge.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, cool. All right, are you done with your design, or is there anything else that you want to add?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, let me think about that and give me just a moment. I mean, again, I've way oversimplified this. But I guess I think if I were to also think about this at a global scale, I mean, Spotify is a global app. And so, it's possible. So, replication—I didn't really talk about replication other than to do the math to say, 'Hey, maybe 3x replication.' And replication, right? Why do we do this? Well, we do it to make sure that we have data available when there's an outage. So, if I were to, you know, indicate three replicas, but that's a little naive. And the same thing for the metadata, of course. But replicating the data is not just for availability and downtime. It's also, you would want to place those replicas closer to where the users are. And so, from a global perspective, you might have music that is more local, like maybe European punk rock is listened to more in Europe. And maybe BTS, because it's Korean pop, is maybe more popular actually in Korea or in Asia. And so, you might want to have the replicas of those songs, and maybe even the metadata, be more locally represented, more locally, so that you can get to it faster. You don't have to cross an ocean to get to the data and to reach it. So, kind of a geo-aware strategy of data placement and possibly replication strategy, that might be a refinement, I think, that I might make to this design to make it just a little bit more performant, effective, etcetera, etcetera. So, yeah, does that make sense?

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I think that's just an interesting point to add. It's a good idea to wrap up your answer by referring back to the requirements laid out at the beginning of the interview and confirming that your design meets them. If you have time, it's always nice to think big and take a quick look at the problem from a different dimension that could make it more complex. Mark did this with his geolocation idea. He didn't go into detail, but another interviewer might have explored this further, and it could have opened up a whole new aspect of the design. Overall, an excellent answer from Mark.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, great job, Mark. I think that was a really good approach. And yeah, how did you feel now, looking back, now the interview is over? Let's say you've walked out the interview room and let out a big sigh of relief. How are you feeling that it's gone?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I feel pretty good about it. It's, yeah, it's definitely different being on the other side of things. And I know that I'm sure I missed things, and I'm, you know, people are watching this and wind up scheduling a session with me. I'm happy to take the feedback on some things that I missed. I'm sure I've missed things, but yeah, it's fun to do this though.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Cool, good stuff. Well, yeah, thanks very much, and thanks everyone for watching. I really hope you found that useful. If you did, you can like and subscribe, and why not come visit us at igotanoffer.com? There you can find more videos, useful frameworks, and question guides, all completely free. And you can also book expert feedback one-to-one with our coaches from Google, Meta, Amazon, etc. Thank you and good luck with your interview. All right.

training_pairs.question: How would you design Spotify?
training_pairs.answer: Okay, so yeah, Spotify is probably one of the most popular music streaming services out there, I guess. So, let me think about that just for a moment. Because there are lots, there are a lot of aspects of Spotify here. Let me see if I can actually, maybe I'll just take some notes here so that I can kind of think through this, if that's okay. And can I make some assumptions? So, I have my notes. I'll just put this right in here, and then if it's okay, I'll just use this for notes as well as sort of the design diagrams and things like that later on. So yeah, let's talk about some different aspects of Spotify. For Spotify, I can think of things. I mean, obviously, there are songs, music—that's the main thing. There are playlists, users, artists. I guess Spotify even has things like podcasts that you can listen to and things like that. So, there's a lot to it. In terms of the system design for today, can we constrain this a little bit just so that we can actually get through a design? What would be good to focus on here?
training_pairs.follow_up: Can we constrain this a little bit just so that we can actually get through a design? What would be good to focus on here?

training_pairs.question: What would be good to focus on here?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, let's constrain it. Let's limit ourselves to finding and playing music. Okay?
training_pairs.follow_up: Okay, one billion users. Okay, what about the number of songs?

training_pairs.question: What about the number of songs?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, I think I saw it somewhere that Spotify has about 80 million songs, but let's say we need to have capacity for 100 million on there. Okay, all right. So, 100 million songs, a billion users, and we're going to focus on the song piece of it. But let me do a little bit of math here, or a little bit of thinking, and I'll just double-check with you to make sure that this makes sense. From what I know, a typical MP3 audio for a typical song is about five megabytes. I mean, I think that depends on the encoding of the song, how long the song is, and things like that. But if that's okay, we can just kind of make that assumption. Is that fair?

training_pairs.question: Is that fair?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, that sounds good to me. Yeah, I mean, I think you can encode these things at really low quality, like 96 kilobits per second, all the way up to 320 kilobits. But right in the middle, 128-160 is about maybe about that. So, all right, let's assume that. And so then, let me just extrapolate from this. If you've got five megabytes per song, you've got 100 million songs. So, I think that translates to—I'm trying to do my math here—100 million would be going to a thousand times that would be 100 billion, and then 100 trillion would be a million times times five would be 500 trillion. So, we're talking about total audio is something like 500 terabytes, which is, I guess, the same thing as half a petabyte of data. And so that's, I'm making that assumption. And so then, depending on how you replicate this data, because you typically want to have these songs in multiple, have multiple copies of these things so they don't get lost, etc., and if some replica is down. So, maybe you do, let's say, 3x replication. So, that would mean like one and a half petabytes of raw audio data. And then, each song in terms of the metadata, meaning the song title and things like that, and artists and all these things, the metadata is probably not very big. It might be, I don't know, 100 bytes per song or something like that per song of metadata. And so, you might wind up with, okay, so let me do the—you have it, and that's a billion, 10 billion. So, that's only 10 gigabytes of song metadata. It's not really very much. And even if you say it's a kilobyte, then that's 100 gigabytes. So, it's not very much. And let me just double-check my math here: 100 million times 100, really 100 bytes? Yeah, I think that would be 10 gigabytes. So, even if we said, if we rounded it up dramatically and said 100 gigabytes, that's just not a ton of data. Now, in terms of users, user data, you might have, again, maybe you have a kilobyte per user metadata. I guess it's all metadata. And so, that might be times a billion users, so that would be a terabyte of data approximately. So, this is just me doing some quick metrics, quick calculations to get a rough back-of-the-envelope of how big these things are. So, does that make sense so far? Does that seem?
training_pairs.follow_up: Does that make sense so far? Does that seem?

training_pairs.question: Does that make sense so far? Does that seem?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, that all makes sense so far. Okay, so let me maybe think about some basic components here, and I'm going to draw them, but I'll get into them maybe a little bit more in detail later. Okay, so some basic components that I would see. Let me start with something that is my best attempt at a mobile phone here. So, you've got your Spotify app, and I'll make this smaller so it fits into the thing. So, you've got your app, which is on your phone typically, and I'm going to assume here that we're talking about the phone app because that's very common. And so, you've got your phone. The phone is going to be talking to an application server, a Spotify application server somewhere, or web server, I guess. And so, let's draw some. Let me just put a like over here, just say there's a Spotify web server. And there's not going to just be one; there's going to be lots of them, and assume that that's a lot. And then, of course, just because this is a standard thing that you do when you have applications in the cloud here—this is, I guess, part of the cloud—you've got a load balancer. And so, let me draw. Let's assume that we've got an arrow here that's talking. So, the Spotify app is talking through the load balancer ultimately to these web servers. And so, those are some key components here. Let me think about this a little bit further. Yeah, I think that's good so far. Okay, and then, of course, the most important thing, or one of the most important things here, let me see if I can find a—I know that there's a shape here, here we go, that's a good shape. I'm just going to say, call this the database. And let me make this a little bit more. Let's see, that's what I wanted. Okay, so there's a database. And so, the web servers are going to be talking to the database and basically reading information, writing information to and from the database. So, those are kind of the very high-level components. And I probably am thinking about this. I think this is obviously oversimplified, and I'm not going to redesign Spotify, you know, the existing full application as it exists. But I do think I can get a little bit more detailed here. I'm thinking about the data again because the data is important. So, I'm thinking about the metadata and the user data and the audio data, and they're very different types of data. So, I'm going to actually split this database here into two things, and I'll explain that, or I'll try to explain them just a little bit. Come on, there we go, make this just a little bit more. So, I'm going to split this up and say that there's a song audio database and then there's going to be a metadata database. So, this is users, songs, what else? I mean, ultimately, there'll probably be artists and etcetera, things like that, but we're focusing on the songs, I think, and the music and so on. This is also a database, of course, song audio database and metadata. And so, I think, let me make this a little bit more. This Spotify web server is actually going to talk to this, to the meta—oops—the metadata and the audio database. It's actually going to be talking to two databases here.
training_pairs.follow_up: Can you go into a bit more detail about why you'd split them into two?

training_pairs.question: Can you go into a bit more detail about why you'd split them into two?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, I'm kind of making that assumption. So, okay, let me try and think of this in terms of the technologies that I might use. I might use something like Amazon S3 here, and I might use Amazon RDS. And so now, let me explain what I mean by that. The type of data that the actual MP3 files—those I think of as immutable data. They're just blobs of data, they're files, they're five megabytes roughly on average. And they're not going to change; they're just being streamed, they're being stored and streamed essentially. And so, a blob database, which is what S3 is, lends itself really well towards that. And it could be something like Google Drive, or there are other technologies that are sort of just document-like blob storage systems. But Amazon S3 lends itself really well to that, and it scales greatly. You can just add more and more and more, and it scales linearly, which is great. And so, then you can connect it up to be able to stream the data and things like that. So, that type of data and the access patterns for that, which is really mostly read—you're just streaming this data, you're never going back and forth and writing to it—that would be why I would want to put the songs in that kind of a database. Now, S3 is great for that, works really well. But the metadata, which is the users and their information, and the songs and their information, and maybe they get updated, maybe you're searching across them and having to do queries to find songs for a particular genre or artists or things like that, or you're trying to update your users—like if I'm playing a song and I want to remember where I left off so that when I continue playing it, it remembers that—I'm going to be modifying the data quite a bit or going back and forth between that database and doing these queries. And you can't really do queries over S3, but you can over like a relational database. So, MySQL would be another option. I just chose RDS here, really Amazon's relational database, because I'm going with the AWS family here. But yeah, so let me try and just write something here a little bit more in terms of songs. You would have things like a song ID, you would have a song, maybe a URL like that you'd use for sharing, you would have an artist, a genre, maybe there's a link to album cover, and maybe there's the link to the audio, I guess. So, this is too big to fit. So, this is the type of data that would be stored in—let me draw this in here again—like in that database. And then just the raw—I mean, I almost don't need to write this here—so, song MP3 would be stored in this database down here. So, yeah, I think that the access patterns, the types of things that you want to do, the size of the data—because the size of the data here, I think we said, was going to be, well, half a petabyte for one replica—so S3 lends itself well to that. The data here is going to be in the maybe terabyte range, something like that, and it's going to have lots of queries over it, maybe some updates and things like that. So, that's why I would separate these out, is because I think the access patterns, the size of the data, and the type of queries that you need to be able to do over would be very different. Does that make sense?
training_pairs.follow_up: Does that make sense in terms of finding music?

training_pairs.question: Does that make sense in terms of finding music?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, that makes sense. That's good. Okay, and so now I have a list of songs, and maybe one of the ones in that list is like, 'Oh yeah, that's what I was looking for.' And so, I click on that song to play it. I want to hear that song. So, that's that second part of the use case that we talked about, which is playing music. So, now to play music, this gets a little interesting, and this is, I think, I'm going to wind up maybe changing what I'm thinking about here a little bit. But let me just talk through it. So, I click on the play button, or I click on the song to play it, and that translates into a request from the app to the web server to start playing a song. It has an ID, like I mentioned, an ID in it. And so now, the web server, based on that song information ID, maybe has to go to this database here to look up the link—what did I call it? Oh, an audio link. Maybe that's an MP3 link, I don't know, something like that, that's a better term for it, I don't know. So, the MP3 link or audio link would be returned. And now, the Spotify web server has to go to the database where the actual audio is stored and fetch that database. Now, you could imagine streaming that five megabytes from this database, so chunk by chunk it comes back, and chunk by chunk it goes up to the Spotify app. In order to do this, you need like a WebSocket connection, so you need a kind of a long-standing connection between the application and this web server so that you can send the data back in chunks. But I'm not sure whether I would need to do that, or whether, because it's only five megabytes, that's possibly small enough to just fit in memory, you know, read from S3 and fit into memory, and then have that particular web server chunk it back from there. I think that might be better because it would eliminate the possible lag between the database, so you don't start streaming the audio until you have it in memory. That might be something that I would consider doing. So now, let me think about, so that would be—does that make sense in terms of the playing? Like, you start playing, and obviously, you can control it, you can pause playing, and so on. But does that make sense? So, you start playing, the web server gets the request to play it, maybe gets the information about where to go over here to get the S3 audio storage, reads that back. It's only five megabytes; that should not take—it should be almost instantaneous. And then it starts streaming that back to the application. Does that make sense?

training_pairs.question: Does that make sense?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, that makes sense. Please carry on. Yeah, so all good so far. Okay, so this almost sounds too good to be true, or sounds too easy. I think it's got to be more complicated than that. Okay, so one thing I'm just realizing here is, probably out of these, what did we say? We said 100 million songs. There's probably a lot of stuff that is—I'm going to use the term 'indie artists' or something like that—stuff that few people, not very many people, listen to or isn't very popular. And on the flip side of that, I'm thinking about what could go wrong here. Well, if, like BTS, which is a Korean pop band, if they, let's say, they release a new song, and it's like, 'Hey, everybody wants to listen to this, this is super, oh, have you heard the latest song?' etc. If you've got all of these web servers all fetching that same thing, that same song, and let's say you've got requests like it's released, and in the next minute, you've got, I don't know, how many? You said a billion users. Okay, let's say that 10 million users all request the song all at the same time or something like that because it's just been released, and they're following social media. You could easily overload, in terms of bottlenecks, you could possibly overload that bucket, or that bucket, excuse me, that particular song, that file, or whatever, however it's stored in AWS. And it could be stored different ways. I'm using S3 here. You could overload AWS or S3 there, and you could also possibly be, you know, loading up all these web servers with the same song, streaming the same thing back. So, it's a lot of bandwidth, etc. So, a better thing to do, and a common thing to do for stuff like this, is to use what's called a CDN, Content Delivery Network, which is like a cache. So, this helps reduce the amount of load on backends. So, let me draw something here. Let me just—I am going to pick what shape am I going to pick? I'll just pick randomly something like this. So, maybe over here, so I'm going to say here this is a CDN, which is a song audio cache. This is typing in. And so, what would happen? So, and by the way, the technologies here, just to—this CDN, a Content Delivery Network, is usually very, very close in terms of number of hops and network connections to users. So, what would happen is, let me draw another arrow here, and let me draw an arrow down to here. So, what would happen here is that the first time that this song, this new BTS newly released single, is requested, the web server would read it and stream it like normal. But probably, we need to make sure that these Spotify web servers are somewhat—they're keeping track of things, and they're keeping track of, you know, which songs are being requested, which ones are hot. They probably have a heat map, the most recently requested songs. And so, in that heat map, as they see, 'Oh, this song is now, I've seen this requested the fifth, third time in a minute or something like that,' at that point in time, what it might do is it might actually, instead of just streaming it back to itself, or copying it back to itself, excuse me, it might actually load that into the CDN. And so, I am trying to—I'm going to have to draw another arrow, I think, in order to do that, because these things probably have to talk to the CDN. And by the way, I'm not a super expert on this area in terms of how this works, but there's some connection between this edge caching, this Content Delivery Network, which is just there for caching, and these Spotify web servers. So, these web servers are going to somehow notify the CDN, 'Hey, you should be pulling this song, this BTS latest song. Pull that from the MP3, the audio storage.' So, the CDN would load that up. And this now has to work in conjunction with the application. So, that means that the application, when the person decides, when I request the song, 'I want to play this latest BTS song,' and I haven't had it on my phone yet, it's just been released, before I go and I talk to the web server, I might even go and check in this—the application might actually go and check in this Content Delivery Network to see, 'Is it there?' It might also be so, and if it is there, then I just read it from there. The other option, or the possibility—and again, this is technology that I'm sort of not super familiar with—is that it goes and asks the web server just like it normally would, and the web server sends back a redirect saying, 'Hey, I don't have it, but you should go over here to this Content Delivery Network, this edge cache, to fetch the song. It's much closer to you, you'll get better performance, and you won't be loading me up so much.' So, I've got a lot of arrows here, but the standard flow for a song, first time around, is coming around, getting the metadata, going around to reading the audio data. The web server is now keeping track and realizing, 'Oh, you know what, this is getting—I'm getting multiple requests for this same song.' Now, I'm going to tell the CDN, 'Go and fetch this song.' It's going to fetch it. And so now, the application can be redirected, for example, to read that audio from there. So, that's a lot of talking, but I was realizing that caching here seems like it's a very important point in this design and would help with the bottleneck of hot songs, if you will. Does that, is that?

training_pairs.question: Does that, is that?
training_pairs.answer: Absolutely. Yeah, by the way, that's a really good answer. Okay, and there's, again, in the AWS family here—I'm going to use the family—obviously, if I were a Googler, I might not be using this. But there's a technology called CloudFront, and CloudFront is basically a Content Delivery Network. There are a bunch of other ones—gosh, Fastly, is that what it is? I can't remember what the names of these technologies are, but there are a bunch of other technologies that are very similar in nature that allow you to do this. So, that would help with the bottlenecks. And in terms of the caching here, you could also—and this is part of the reason, by the way, I'm just stepping backwards here a little bit—part of the reason why I think it would be better initially when the web server is getting the song for the first time, for it to read the entire MP3 into its memory. Because then, if it is getting multiple requests for a particular song, then it doesn't have to go to the database; it can actually just feed it from its memory. And that's also a form of caching, of course. So, it's got a local cache essentially of these songs. And you could imagine having a shared cache that's shared across these web servers, so that could be another optimization. But my point is that if we have a cache in memory in these web servers that stores the songs, then we're offloading the database a little bit. If we then have the cache here at the edge of the network—so this is, that's what it's called, edge network, edge caching—then we're offloading the web servers to a large extent because the streaming is optimized for streaming and so on. And I think if we go one step further, actually, I'm sure, and if I were designing it, I would design the application, because these are on smartphones, to store the songs that are played frequently by the specific user locally in the local storage. So, it's another form of caching. So then, if it finds the song in its local cache, essentially local store here on the phone, then you wouldn't even need to go to the network at all, right? So, you could actually—I'll even draw like this—so it's like a little cache, a little local storage here of songs. So, there's multi-layer caching, I guess what I'm saying, multiple levels of caching, ultimately with the goal to provide the best user experience, make sure it's super fast, and to then offload obviously the system to allow it to scale and limit costs, etc. So, that's probably enough on caching, and I think I've gone deep a little bit there.
training_pairs.follow_up: How would you think about load balancing for this particular app?

training_pairs.question: How would you think about load balancing for this particular app?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, magic load balancing. It's, yeah, a little bit hand-wavy here. I think of load balancing—I mean, so load balancing commonly is used to—the load balancer's job here really is to make sure that these web servers are not overloaded so that they're providing good service to the end users and they're not overloaded. And overloaded can mean many things. Often, it's in terms of CPU. So, you're getting lots of requests coming in, and if you're getting lots of requests coming in, then just load balancing these requests across the servers so that there's roughly the same number of requests on each one, assuming they're all equal, would probably also result in the CPU utilization being about equal. I think for this application, I would think about load balancing, I might think about it a little bit differently because we're streaming data. And so, instead of using CPU as my load balancing metric to figure out how to distribute the load, I might be looking at possibly network bandwidth. Because if a particular web server is not doing a lot from a CPU perspective, but if it is I/O bound or network bound, meaning it's hitting its limit of its network connection, then I wouldn't want the load balancer to send it more traffic because it's just going to bog down, and you're going to get skips and things like that. So, I might make this load balancer aware of multiple metrics, one of them being network, maybe memory for caching, although you can kind of limit that possibly, but maybe not CPU for this purpose. It might be requests outstanding or current streams or something like that. There might be some other metrics, but I would want to make this load balancer maybe a little bit smarter than just a typical request round-robin load balancing scheme. So, I'm not sure if that's getting at what you're asking.
training_pairs.follow_up: Are you done with your design, or is there anything else that you want to add?

training_pairs.question: Are you done with your design, or is there anything else that you want to add?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, let me think about that and give me just a moment. I mean, again, I've way oversimplified this. But I guess I think if I were to also think about this at a global scale, I mean, Spotify is a global app. And so, it's possible. So, replication—I didn't really talk about replication other than to do the math to say, 'Hey, maybe 3x replication.' And replication, right? Why do we do this? Well, we do it to make sure that we have data available when there's an outage. So, if I were to, you know, indicate three replicas, but that's a little naive. And the same thing for the metadata, of course. But replicating the data is not just for availability and downtime. It's also, you would want to place those replicas closer to where the users are. And so, from a global perspective, you might have music that is more local, like maybe European punk rock is listened to more in Europe. And maybe BTS, because it's Korean pop, is maybe more popular actually in Korea or in Asia. And so, you might want to have the replicas of those songs, and maybe even the metadata, be more locally represented, more locally, so that you can get to it faster. You don't have to cross an ocean to get to the data and to reach it. So, kind of a geo-aware strategy of data placement and possibly replication strategy, that might be a refinement, I think, that I might make to this design to make it just a little bit more performant, effective, etcetera, etcetera. So, yeah, does that make sense?

training_pairs.question: Does that make sense?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, I think that's just an interesting point to add.
training_pairs.follow_up: How did you feel now, looking back, now the interview is over?

training_pairs.question: How did you feel now, looking back, now the interview is over?
training_pairs.answer: I feel pretty good about it. It's, yeah, it's definitely different being on the other side of things. And I know that I'm sure I missed things, and I'm, you know, people are watching this and wind up scheduling a session with me. I'm happy to take the feedback on some things that I missed. I'm sure I've missed things, but yeah, it's fun to do this though.
